topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips. It was tucked inside a dusty old book, a forgotten relic from my grandmother’s attic. Unfurling the yellowed parchment, I saw the familiar, looping script of my father, the ink faded but still legible. A resignation. Dated nineteen-seventy-eight. My chest tightened, a sudden, sharp constriction that made my breath hitch. I reread the words: "Effective immediately, I resign from all duties." A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a sound that wasn't quite a sob, more like grinding stone. I crumpled the letter, the sound of the tearing paper echoing too loudly in the quiet house. Why did he just… walk away? Decades of unspoken questions clawed at the edges of my composure, turning my hands into useless, shaking claws. I threw the crumpled mess against the wall, the impact rattling a nearby glass.

[story 2]

I found the envelope tucked beneath a stack of old tax documents. It was my mother’s handwriting, elegant and precise, but the contents were a detonation in my quiet afternoon. Inside, a formal letter, a document of professional departure from a company I’d never heard of. Reading the neat lines brought a hot, prickly sensation rising up my neck. My jaw locked so tight I could feel the tendons strain beneath my skin. I paced the length of the living room, my footsteps heavy and uneven on the hardwood floor. Every word felt like a deliberate slight, a calculated abandonment. I stopped abruptly by the window, staring out at the indifferent streetlights, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame. The air in the room felt too thick, suffocating. I wanted to scream, to shatter something, but all that emerged was a strained, shallow gasp, followed by a furious, silent shaking of my entire frame.

[story 3]

It was a slip of paper, discovered accidentally while clearing out a storage box. My father’s handwriting, undeniably. The resignation notice was stark, a declaration of severance from a life I’d always assumed was stable. A slow, hot flush spread across my face, starting at my collarbone and creeping up to my hairline. I held it up to the weak afternoon light, scrutinizing the ink as if it held the key to some terrible secret. A sudden, violent urge to throw it into the nearest receptacle overcame me. My grip tightened until my fingers ached, and I felt a tremor run through my whole body, a frantic, uncontrolled energy buzzing beneath my skin. I didn't throw it; instead, I squeezed my eyes shut, breathing in ragged, sharp intakes of air, trying desperately to contain the sheer, explosive force building behind my ribs. The silence of the room amplified the internal racket.
